file_name: Zhang et al. - 2023 - Identification of different types of tumors based on photoacoustic spectral analysis preclinical fe.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Support Vector Machine (SVM), a machine learning algorithm, to perform automatic classification and diagnosis of skin tumors (squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, and normal tissue) based on photoacoustic spectral analysis parameters. The SVM achieved diagnostic accuracies of 91.7% for normal tissue, 93.3% for SCC, and 91.7% for BCC.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH PAPER' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving ex vivo human tissues, photoacoustic spectral analysis (PASA) measurements, and machine learning classification (SVM) to identify tumor types, including specific methods, results, and statistical analyses.

title: Identification of different types of tumors based on photoacoustic spectral analysis: preclinical feasibility studies on skin tumors
brief_summary: This study investigated the feasibility of using photoacoustic spectral analysis (PASA) to identify different types of skin tumors based on collagen and lipid content in the tumor microenvironment. Ex vivo human tissues from 39 patients, including normal skin, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and basal cell carcinoma (BCC), were analyzed using PASA to assess relative lipid and collagen levels. The extracted PASA parameters were used as input features for a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier with a radial basis function kernel to automatically categorize the tissue types. The SVM model achieved diagnostic accuracies of 91.7% for normal tissue, 93.3% for SCC, and 91.7% for BCC, with areas under the ROC curves of 0.94, 0.96, and 0.92, respectively. The results demonstrated that PASA combined with machine learning can effectively differentiate between normal skin and specific skin cancer types, offering a potential non-invasive diagnostic tool.

lesion_types_studied.details: Ex vivo human normal skin samples collected from skin graft areas of 12 patients; used as a comparator class for tumor identification.
Ex vivo human tissue samples from 15 patients with suspected SCC; one of the primary tumor types classified by the SVM model.
Ex vivo human tissue samples from 12 patients with suspected BCC; one of the primary tumor types classified by the SVM model.

all_task_types.target: Tumor type (Normal tissue, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The study uses a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to perform automatic multiclass categorization of skin tissue types. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (normal, SCC, BCC) based on photoacoustic spectral analysis parameters. Performance was evaluated using diagnostic accuracies (91.7% for normal, 93.3% for SCC, 91.7% for BCC) and ROC curves.

datasets_used.name: Ex vivo human skin tissue cohort
datasets_used.description: A preclinical dataset consisting of ex vivo human skin tissue samples collected from the Institute of Photomedicine, Shanghai Skin Disease Hospital, School of Medicine, Tongji University, China. The samples were obtained from surgical excisions and skin graft areas of 39 patients.

use.training: Used for training the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier; two-thirds of the samples were used for training in each fold of the three-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter selection (C and gamma) via grid searching with cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing the SVM classifier; the remaining one-third of samples were used for prediction in each fold of the three-fold cross-validation.
use.other: Used for histopathological validation (H&E, Masson’s trichrome, Nile red staining) to corroborate photoacoustic spectral analysis (PASA) results regarding lipid and collagen content.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To examine whether the changes in collagen and lipid content of the tumor microenvironment in different types of skin tissues were statistically significant.

To select optimal hyperparameters (regularization parameter C and kernel parameter gamma) for the Support Vector Machine classifier by testing various pairs of values and selecting the pair with the best accuracy.
To increase classification reliability by rotating datasets used to train and test the SVM model, ensuring each group was tested once.
To evaluate the performance of the classifier model, including accurate classification and misclassification rates.
To evaluate the performance of the classifier model and calculate the area under the curve (AUC) for normal, SCC, and BCC tissues.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in collagen and lipid content of the tumor microenvironment between different types of skin tissues (e.g., normal vs. tumor, or SCC vs. BCC).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in collagen and lipid content of the tumor microenvironment between different types of skin tissues.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined using p-values, with thresholds of p < 0.05, p < 0.01, and p < 0.001 indicating significant differences in lipid and collagen content between tissue types, consistent with histopathological results.

Ex vivo human skin tissue cohort (39 sam.metric: Statistical Significance of Lipid and Collagen Content (PASA vs Histology)
Ex vivo human skin tissue cohort (39 sam.notes: PASA results showed lipid and collagen levels in tumors were significantly lower than in normal tissue. There was a statistical difference between SCC and BCC (p < 0.05), consistent with histopathological results. Normal tissue had well-distributed and parallel collagen, while tumor tissues had unorderly and reduced collagen.

architecture_used.input_size: 21 features (relative APSD values at three wave bands: 1200-1240 nm, 1600-1700 nm, and 1300-1340 nm)

hyperparameters.regularization_parameter_C: Selected via grid searching with cross-validation
hyperparameters.kernel_parameter_gamma: Selected via grid searching with cross-validation

training_details.data_split: Two-thirds of samples used for training, one-third for testing in each fold
training_details.hyperparameter_selection: Grid searching with cross-validation to select optimal (C, γ) pair

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: C-type SVM with radial basis function (RBF) kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Cleaning residual blood on tissue surface with sterile gauze
Calibration of PA signals using peak-to-peak value of PA signal generated by black body at each wavelength
Calculation of PA signal power spectra using Welch’s method with a 5 μs moving Hamming window and 60% overlap
Analysis of ultrasound frequency in the 1 to 8 MHz range
Combination of power spectra from 1200 to 1700 nm wavelength range to form PA physiochemical spectrogram (PAPCS)
Calculation of relative area of power spectrum density (APSD) at corresponding wavelengths
Averaging relative APSD obtained at absorption wavelength ranges of lipid or collagen to reduce measurement error
Random division of samples into two groups (two-thirds for training, one-third for testing) for three-fold cross-validation
conclusions: Collagen and lipid levels in the tumor microenvironment are significantly lower than in normal tissue, with a statistical difference between squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and basal cell carcinoma (BCC) (p < 0.05), consistent with histopathological results.

Photoacoustic spectral analysis (PASA) combined with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier can effectively identify different types of skin tumors, achieving diagnostic accuracies of 91.7% for normal tissue, 93.3% for SCC, and 91.7% for BCC.
The proposed PASA method provides a new, non-invasive way to diagnose tumors by characterizing tumor-related features based on collagen and lipid content.
The study is limited by the small sample size of ex vivo human tissues; future work should include more training datasets and higher K-fold cross-validation to improve model robustness.
In vivo application of PASA requires spectral unmixing procedures to account for the high optical absorption of water in the 1200 to 1700 nm wavelength range.
The approach has potential to be extended to the diagnosis of other cancers with altered collagen or lipid contents, such as breast and prostate cancer.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size (n=39), which the authors acknowledge as a constraint for model robustness.
The dataset consists exclusively of ex vivo human tissues, limiting the immediate clinical applicability and generalizability to in vivo conditions.
The paper explicitly notes that in vivo application requires spectral unmixing procedures to account for the high optical absorption of water in the 1200 to 1700 nm wavelength range.
The SVM classifier was trained and tested using three-fold cross-validation on a single institution's dataset, raising potential concerns regarding external validation and dataset bias.
The study relies on semi-quantitative PASA parameters validated against histology, but does not report confidence intervals for the diagnostic accuracy metrics.
